abstract.t: Vanta is a high-performance binary protocol for secure, verifiable data exchange across heterogeneous transports. It supports client/server, peer-to-peer, relay-routed, pub/sub, and streaming communication patterns.
Vanta emphasizes minimal latency, strong cryptographic identity, effectively-once mutation semantics, schema evolution, capability negotiation, and cryptographically verifiable audit chains.

section.name: Introduction
section.t: Modern distributed systems often need to combine high throughput, low latency, cryptographic authentication, protocol evolution, and verifiable execution behavior. Existing protocols usually solve only part of that problem space.
Vanta is designed as a binary protocol for secure, efficient, transport-agnostic communication with protocol-native auditing and explicit operational semantics.

section.name: Terminology
section.t: The key words "MUST", "MUST NOT", "REQUIRED", "SHALL", "SHALL NOT", "SHOULD", "SHOULD NOT", "RECOMMENDED", "MAY", and "OPTIONAL" in this document are to be interpreted as described in RFC 2119 and RFC 8174 when, and only when, they appear in all capitals.
The following protocol terms are used throughout this document:

li.t: Peer: A protocol participant implementing Vanta.
Session: A cryptographically authenticated communication channel established between two peers.
Stream: A logical communication channel multiplexed inside a session.
Frame: A single Vanta protocol unit transmitted over a transport.
Mutation: An operation that modifies application state.
Audit Receipt: A signed record describing a protocol event.

section.name: Protocol Overview
section.t: Vanta is structured into four logical layers:

li.t: VSP: secure session establishment and transport protection

section.t: Vanta is intended to operate over reliable transports including TCP, WebSocket, and Unix domain sockets, and can also be routed through blind relay nodes.

section.name: Design Goals
section.t: Vanta has the following primary design goals:

li.t: strong security with low cryptographic overhead

section.name: Layer Model
section.t: Conceptually, the protocol stack is:
section.sourcecode: Application ^ VAP - Application Payload Layer ^ VCP - Capability and Schema Negotiation ^ VSP - Secure Session Layer ^ VTP - Framing and Multiplexing Layer ^ Transport (TCP / WebSocket / Unix Socket / Relay)

section.name: Core Identifiers
section.t: Vanta defines a number of fixed-width identifiers.

section.name: Transport Requirements
section.t: The underlying transport MUST provide reliable delivery. It MAY provide either a continuous ordered byte stream or message boundaries.
The initial intended transports are TCP, WebSocket, and Unix domain sockets.
A relay transport MAY forward Vanta traffic, but a blind relay MUST NOT decrypt end-to-end protected payloads and MUST NOT modify protected frame contents.

section.name: Message Framing (VTP)
section.t: All Vanta communication occurs through binary frames. Each frame consists of:
All fixed-width integers are encoded in network byte order (big-endian).

section.name: Base Header Layout
section.t: The Vanta base header is fixed at 80 bytes.
Implementations MUST validate header lengths and frame lengths before processing payload data.

tr.td: Reserved; MUST be zero in v0

section.name: Extension Block
section.t: Frames MAY contain extension fields using a compact TLV encoding. In v0, extension items use a one-byte type field, one-byte length field, and a variable-length value.
Unknown optional extension types MAY be ignored according to negotiated extension policy. Unknown mandatory extension types MUST cause frame rejection.

section.name: Secure Session Protocol (VSP)
section.t: Session establishment proceeds in the following phases:
A session MUST NOT carry application data until the handshake and negotiation phases are complete.

section.name: Cryptographic Suite
section.t: The default Vanta v0 cryptographic suite is:

section.name: Peer Identity
section.t: Each peer has a long-term Ed25519 identity key pair. The public key bytes constitute the PeerID.
Trust establishment is out of scope for the wire protocol. Implementations MAY use pinned keys, centralized key registries, or equivalent trust bootstrap mechanisms.

section.name: Key Derivation
section.t: After authenticated exchange of ephemeral X25519 public keys, both peers derive a shared Diffie-Hellman secret and use HKDF-SHA-256 to derive directional session keys, nonce bases, a session identifier, and optional resumption material.
A Vanta implementation MUST NOT reuse an AEAD nonce under the same session key.

section.name: Capability and Schema Negotiation (VCP)
section.t: During negotiation, peers exchange supported schema families, schema versions, protocol capabilities, and extension namespaces.
A capability or extension MUST NOT be used unless it has been positively negotiated.
Vanta schemas are intended to support optional fields, unknown field tolerance, and explicit version tracking.

section.name: Message Semantics (VAP)
section.t: Vanta supports request/response, commands, events, and streams.

section.name: Request / Response
section.t: Requests and responses are linked using CorrelationID where applicable.

section.name: Commands
section.t: Mutating operations are carried in COMMAND frames. A command MUST include MessageID, OperationID, and an idempotency token or equivalent deduplication key.

section.name: Events
section.t: EVENT frames are used for pub/sub and asynchronous signaling.

section.name: Streams
section.t: Large or continuous data transfer uses STREAM_OPEN, STREAM_DATA, and STREAM_CLOSE frames.

section.name: Control Frames and Reliability
section.t: Vanta distinguishes a control plane from the data plane. Control frames are used for acknowledgements, flow control, heartbeats, error reporting, session coordination, and audit receipts.
Control-plane traffic SHOULD be prioritized over application data traffic.

section.name: Acknowledgements
section.t: Vanta distinguishes transport acknowledgement from semantic acknowledgement.

li.t: Transport acknowledgement confirms that a frame was received and parsed at the protocol layer.
Semantic acknowledgement confirms the result of application handling, such as mutation application or rejection.

section.name: Flow Control
section.t: Flow control is receiver-driven. The receiver grants credits that limit outstanding bytes and, optionally, message counts.
A sender MUST stop sending data-plane traffic when its credits are exhausted, except where control-plane reservation rules apply.

section.name: Effectively-Once Mutation Semantics
section.t: Vanta targets effectively-once semantics for mutations. Duplicate detection is based on sender identity and mutation identifiers, not on transport delivery alone.
A receiver SHOULD maintain a deduplication cache for the active session and return an explicit semantic disposition such as:

section.name: Retransmission
section.t: Retransmission is explicit. A retransmitted frame MUST preserve the original MessageID and any relevant OperationID.
A retransmitted frame SHOULD set the RETRANSMIT flag.

section.name: Reconnection and Failure Semantics
section.t: After a transport failure, a peer MAY reconnect using protocol policy and optional resume support.
A mutation whose semantic result is not known at disconnect time MUST be treated as UNKNOWN_AFTER_DISCONNECT until reconciled by retry, state query, or application-level recovery.

section.name: Audit Chain and Verifiable Execution Model
section.t: Vanta includes a protocol-native audit system for important events including mutation handling, validation failures, retransmissions, and session milestones.
Audit receipts are designed to be compact, deterministic, cryptographically signed, and tamper-evident.

section.name: Audit Receipt Format
section.t: An AUDIT_RECEIPT frame contains, at minimum, a receipt identifier, a previous receipt hash, a session identifier, message and operation identifiers where applicable, sender and receiver peer identifiers, an event type, a disposition, a payload hash, a timestamp, and a receiver signature.
Audit records MUST NOT include plaintext application payloads.

section.name: Audit Chain Construction
section.t: Audit receipts form an append-only hash chain. Each receipt commits to the previous receipt hash.
A verifier can therefore detect missing, modified, or reordered receipts.

section.name: Privacy Properties
section.t: The audit system is intended to preserve confidentiality by storing only metadata and hashes rather than application payloads or query contents.
End-to-end encrypted payloads SHOULD be hashed in ciphertext form for audit binding.

section.name: Protocol Invariants
section.t: The following invariants apply to Vanta v0:

li.t: Every semantic message MUST have a unique MessageID.
Every mutation MUST have an OperationID.
Session encryption is mandatory after handshake completion.
A capability MUST NOT be used before negotiation.
Unknown schema fields SHOULD be ignored according to schema policy.
Silent downgrade of negotiated features MUST NOT occur.
Audit chains MUST be append-only and MUST NOT fork.

section.name: Implementation Notes
section.t: The initial intended implementation targets are Rust, Go, and TypeScript. A Rust implementation is expected to serve as the canonical reference due to its suitability for binary parsing, cryptography, async networking, and correctness-focused protocol engineering.

section.name: Future Work
section.t: Potential future extensions include compression negotiation, QUIC transport support, Merkle-based audit summaries, multi-signer receipts, hardware-backed signing, and zero-knowledge audit proofs.

section.name: Security Considerations
section.t: Implementations MUST verify peer signatures during handshake, MUST bind negotiated protocol properties to the authenticated transcript, and MUST prevent AEAD nonce reuse.
Compromise of a peer's long-term identity key can invalidate authentication and audit attribution for that peer.
Implementations SHOULD use strong randomness for nonces, ephemeral key generation, and identifier generation where unpredictability is required.

section.name: Privacy Considerations
section.t: Even when payloads are encrypted, routing and operational metadata may remain observable to transports or relays, depending on deployment mode.
Implementations SHOULD minimize exposed metadata, especially in relay-routed topologies.

front.title: Key words for use in RFCs to Indicate Requirement Levels
Ambiguity of Uppercase vs Lowercase in RFC 2119 Key Words
ChaCha20 and Poly1305 for IETF Protocols
